pattern: Since only distinct values can ever end up in a continuous array, and any element can be freely rewritten to anything, the problem reduces to finding the largest run of distinct existing values that already fits inside some window of n consecutive integers -- everything outside that run gets rewritten.
hints: Duplicates never help, since a continuous array has all-unique elements -- so first reduce to the distinct values, sorted.
For each distinct value used as the window's lower bound, the window's upper bound is fixed at that value plus n - 1; count how many distinct values fall inside that window.
The largest such count, over every possible starting value, is how many elements you get to keep; the answer is n minus that count, since every other element must be rewritten.

edge_inputs.description: one gap in an otherwise consecutive run
values spread far apart with no overlap at all
a single element is trivially continuous
every element identical, collapsing to one distinct value
the two extreme allowed values together
negative-adjacent-looking values (all far below zero) still form a window
an evenly spaced arithmetic run where every window holds the same count

stress.description: fully random large values across the entire range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 10**9) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: values clustered within a small window of each other, stressing large overlapping counts
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    base = rng.randint(1, 10**9 - 2 * n - 10)
    nums = [base + rng.randint(0, 2 * n) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
